@graph.description: Time is invisible. For many kids, 'five more minutes' means nothing because they can't see or feel time passing. Countdown materials make time visible — and transitions possible. Here are 9 that help.

mainEntity.name: Why does every time i say 'five more minutes,' it's like i've said nothing at all. then when time is actually up, complete meltdown. my daughter has no sense of how long five minutes is. she can't feel time passing. when i say 'we're leaving soon,' she doesn't understand what 'soon' means. one minute? ten minutes? an hour? it's all the same to her. so every transition catches her off guard, even when i've given warnings. i've tried verbal countdowns — 'ten minutes... five minutes... two minutes...' — but the words don't connect to anything real for her. she can't see time, can't feel it moving, can't prepare for what's coming. and then i'm the bad guy for 'suddenly' ending something she was enjoying. the screaming, the crying, the complete inability to shift gears. it happens at home, at the park, at playdates, leaving school. she gets so locked into what she's doing that any change feels like an assault. i've started dreading every transition because i know what's coming. her teachers say she has the same problem at school — circle time ends and she falls apart, recess ends and she can't cope, any unexpected schedule change throws her completely. it's like she needs to mentally prepare for changes, but she can't prepare for something she can't see coming. abstract time warnings don't work. 'almost done' doesn't work. 'a few more turns' doesn't work. she needs to see it somehow. i need something that makes time visible. something that helps her actually see and feel the countdown happening. something that gives her the warning her brain needs to prepare for the shift. because verbal warnings aren't working, and i can't keep living with a meltdown every time anything changes. i'm exhausted. she's exhausted. there has to be a better way?

acceptedAnswer.text: TRANSITION DIFFICULTIES / TIME PERCEPTION DEFICITS / EXECUTIVE FUNCTION CHALLENGES / COGNITIVE INFLEXIBILITY / TIME BLINDNESS / DIFFICULTY WITH TEMPORAL PROCESSING! Countdown strategies are structured approaches to helping children understand, anticipate, and cope with the passage of time and upcoming transitions. Many children, particularly those with autism spectrum disorder, ADHD, anxiety, or executive function challenges, have difficulty with temporal processing — the ability to perceive, estimate, and track the passage of time. This 'time blindness' means that verbal time warnings ('five more minutes') have little meaning because the child cannot connect abstract time units to felt experience. Countdowns make time visible and concrete, providing the advance warning that allows the child's brain to prepare for change. Effective countdown strategies typically involve: visual representation of time passing (timers that show time disappearing), auditory or visual cues at intervals, concrete markers of progress toward transition, and consistent use across settings. When children can SEE time running out, they can begin the mental process of disengaging from the current activity and preparing for the next. Without visible countdowns, transitions feel sudden and jarring — even when verbal warnings were given — because the child's internal sense of time didn't register those warnings. Countdown materials range from simple visual timers to elaborate transition systems, but they share the common goal of making the invisible (time) visible.

mainEntity.name: What is Transition Difficulties / Time Perception Deficits / Executive Function Challenges / Cognitive Inflexibility / Time Blindness / Difficulty with Temporal Processing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Countdown strategies are structured approaches to helping children understand, anticipate, and cope with the passage of time and upcoming transitions. Many children, particularly those with autism spectrum disorder, ADHD, anxiety, or executive function challenges, have difficulty with temporal processing — the ability to perceive, estimate, and track the passage of time. This 'time blindness' means that verbal time warnings ('five more minutes') have little meaning because the child cannot connect abstract time units to felt experience. Countdowns make time visible and concrete, providing the advance warning that allows the child's brain to prepare for change. Effective countdown strategies typically involve: visual representation of time passing (timers that show time disappearing), auditory or visual cues at intervals, concrete markers of progress toward transition, and consistent use across settings. When children can SEE time running out, they can begin the mental process of disengaging from the current activity and preparing for the next. Without visible countdowns, transitions feel sudden and jarring — even when verbal warnings were given — because the child's internal sense of time didn't register those warnings. Countdown materials range from simple visual timers to elaborate transition systems, but they share the common goal of making the invisible (time) visible. Common signs include: Meltdowns when activities end, even with verbal warnings given, No apparent understanding of time concepts ('five minutes,' 'soon,' 'later'), Difficulty stopping preferred activities to move to non-preferred ones, Seems surprised by transitions even when warned repeatedly, Cannot estimate how long activities will take.

mainEntity.name: How does a Visual Timers (Time Timer®, Sand Timers, Visual Countdown Clocks) help with transition difficulties?

acceptedAnswer.text: Visual timers are the foundation of countdown strategies because they make the abstract concrete. The Time Timer® and similar visual timers show time as a colored disk that disappears as time passes — children can literally SEE time running out. Sand timers show sand flowing from one chamber to another, providing both visual and kinesthetic awareness of time passing. Unlike digital clocks that show numbers (abstract), visual timers show quantity of time remaining (concrete). For children with time blindness, this is transformative. When a child can see that the red section is getting smaller, they understand viscerally that the activity is ending soon. Their brain can begin preparing for the transition before it happens, rather than being surprised when time is 'suddenly' up. Visual timers work because they bypass the need for time estimation (a skill the child lacks) and provide direct perception of time remaining. They create predictability without requiring the child to hold abstract time concepts in working memory. The child doesn't need to understand what 'five minutes' means — they just need to watch the color disappear. This simple intervention alone can dramatically reduce transition meltdowns by giving the child's brain the advance warning it needs to shift gears. Price: ₹800–3,000. DIY option: Using visual timers effectively: 1) CHOOSE THE RIGHT TIMER: Time Timer® (classic visual timer with disappearing red disk) is the gold standard; sand timers work well for shorter durations (1-5 minutes) and add sensory element; liquid motion timers for calming visual effect; phone/tablet apps work but dedicated devices avoid screen distractions, 2) MATCH DURATION TO TASK: use appropriate time length — don't set a 30-minute timer for a 5-minute countdown; start with the time that matches the child's tolerance, 3) PLACEMENT: timer should be visible to child during the activity — on the table, on the wall, wherever they can see it while playing, 4) INTRODUCE THE CONCEPT: before using in high-stakes situations, teach what the timer means — 'When the red is gone, we're all done' — practice with preferred activities first, 5) PAIR WITH LANGUAGE: 'Look, the timer shows we have this much time left (pointing). When it's all gone, we'll clean up,' 6) CONSISTENT USE: use the same timer consistently so child learns to trust it; inconsistent use undermines effectiveness, 7) HONOR THE TIMER: when the timer goes off, the activity ends — don't regularly extend time or the timer loses meaning, 8) START WITH PREFERRED-TO-PREFERRED: first practice transitioning from one preferred activity to another; once child trusts the system, use for preferred-to-non-preferred transitions, 9) MULTIPLE WARNINGS: set timer for full duration, then point out time remaining at intervals — 'Look, we have this much left... now this much...', 10) PORTABLE TIMERS: keep a portable timer for use outside the home — parks, stores, playdates. COMMON MISTAKES: setting timer for too long (child forgets about it); using timer inconsistently; not honoring the timer when it ends; using timer only for non-preferred activities (creates negative association). Safety: For some anxious children, watching time run out can increase anxiety rather than reduce it. Monitor child's response and adjust approach if timer creates stress rather than support.

mainEntity.name: How does a First-Then Boards and Visual Schedules help with transition difficulties?

acceptedAnswer.text: First-Then boards show two activities in sequence: 'FIRST [current activity], THEN [next activity].' This simple visual tool helps children understand that the current activity will end AND shows them what comes next. Knowing what's coming reduces the anxiety that often underlies transition resistance. Many children resist transitions not because they can't handle ending the current activity, but because they're anxious about the unknown — what happens after? First-Then boards answer that question visually. Visual schedules extend this concept to show the entire sequence of activities (morning routine, school day, after-school schedule). Children can see where they are in the sequence and what's coming, reducing the surprise factor of transitions. The visual format is critical because it provides concrete, persistent information — unlike verbal instructions that disappear after being spoken. A child can look at the First-Then board repeatedly, referencing it to prepare for the transition. For countdown purposes, First-Then boards are often paired with visual timers: 'First we play for this much time (timer), THEN we have snack.' The combination provides both the 'when' (timer) and the 'what's next' (First-Then board), giving the child complete transition information. Price: ₹300–1,500. DIY option: Creating and using First-Then boards: 1) MAKE THE BOARD: simple board with two sections labeled 'FIRST' and 'THEN'; can use whiteboard, laminated card, felt board, or purchased board; Velcro spots for interchangeable pictures, 2) PICTURE CARDS: create picture cards for common activities — playing, eating, bathing, school, outside, TV, reading, etc.; use real photos for younger children or those who need concrete images; simple icons work for older children, 3) SET UP BEFORE TRANSITION: place First-Then board before the current activity begins; child sees from the start what's coming, 4) REVIEW VERBALLY: 'Look at your board. FIRST we're playing with blocks. THEN we're having snack,' 5) REFERENCE DURING COUNTDOWN: 'The timer shows we have this much time for FIRST. Then we'll do THEN,' 6) MARK COMPLETION: when first activity ends, move or remove the FIRST picture; visually mark the transition happening, 7) MOTIVATING 'THEN': especially for difficult transitions, make THEN something motivating — 'FIRST cleanup, THEN iPad time,' 8) CONSISTENT PLACEMENT: keep board in consistent location so child knows where to look, 9) EXPAND TO SCHEDULES: as child develops, expand to visual schedules showing more activities in sequence; use for daily routines, school day, weekend plans, 10) PORTABLE VERSION: create small, portable First-Then board for use outside home. PAIRING WITH COUNTDOWN: First-Then shows WHAT; visual timer shows WHEN. Together they provide complete transition information. 'FIRST play (timer shows 5 minutes). THEN snack.' Safety: If THEN activity is aversive (e.g., doctor appointment), child may resist the entire sequence. For challenging THEN activities, use smaller First-Then chains and add motivators after the hard activity.

mainEntity.name: How does a Countdown Visual Strips and Number Countdowns help with transition difficulties?

acceptedAnswer.text: Countdown visual strips provide a structured visual sequence that marks progress toward transition. They might show numbers (5-4-3-2-1-Done), pictures (5 turns left, 4 turns left...), or other visual markers that the child removes or marks off as the countdown progresses. For some children, visual timers that show continuous time (like the Time Timer) are less effective than discrete countdowns — seeing specific steps toward the end rather than a gradual disappearing segment. Countdown strips make each step distinct: 'Here's 5. Now I'm removing 5. Here's 4. Now I'm removing 4...' The child participates in the countdown by removing numbers or marking off steps, which increases engagement and gives them a sense of control over the transition process. This participatory element is key — the child isn't just watching time happen to them, they're actively participating in the countdown. Countdown strips can represent time (each number = 1 minute), turns (each number = 1 turn in a game), or sequences (5 more swings, 4 more swings...). The flexibility allows matching to the child's understanding — some children grasp 'turns' better than 'minutes.' The visual-motor act of removing cards or marking off numbers also provides a concrete, memorable marker of progress. Price: ₹200–800. DIY option: Creating and using countdown strips: 1) MAKE THE STRIP: laminated strip with Velcro spaces for numbers or pictures; numbers 5-4-3-2-1 plus 'ALL DONE' or 'FINISHED', 2) VELCRO NUMBERS: individual number cards that attach to the strip and can be removed one by one, 3) DETERMINE WHAT EACH NUMBER MEANS: time-based (each number = 1 minute); turn-based (each number = 1 turn); item-based (5 more crackers, 4 more crackers...); the most effective choice depends on the child's understanding, 4) INTRODUCE THE SYSTEM: 'We're going to count down. When all the numbers are gone, we're all done and we'll go inside,' 5) CHILD REMOVES NUMBERS: at each countdown point, the child removes the number from the strip; the active participation increases investment in the process, 6) VERBAL PAIRING: 'That's 5. Now let's take off 5. Now we have 4-3-2-1 left,' 7) CLEAR ENDING: when all numbers are removed, the strip shows 'ALL DONE' or is empty; this visual endpoint is unambiguous, 8) ADJUST LENGTH: start with shorter countdowns (3 or 5) and extend as child builds tolerance; some children need 10-step countdowns for longer activities, 9) PAIR WITH FIRST-THEN: countdown shows WHEN we're done; First-Then shows WHAT'S NEXT, 10) TURN-BASED COUNTDOWNS: for activities where time doesn't apply well (games, swings), countdown turns instead — '5 more turns, 4 more turns...'. VARIATIONS: instead of numbers, use pictures (5 smiley faces disappearing); use tokens child drops in a container; use a path where figure moves toward 'finish.' Key is discrete, visual progress toward defined endpoint. Safety: Some children become anxious watching numbers count down and may resist participation. If this occurs, consider whether continuous visual timers work better than discrete countdowns for this particular child.

mainEntity.name: How does a Transition Songs and Audio Countdown Cues help with transition difficulties?

acceptedAnswer.text: Some children respond better to auditory countdown cues than visual ones — or benefit from having both. Transition songs are consistent musical cues that signal the approach of a transition or mark the transition itself. The famous 'Clean Up Song' is an example of a transition song that signals activity ending. Audio countdowns can include: songs with built-in countdowns ('5 little monkeys' counting down), verbal countdown routines set to music, audio timers that give periodic warnings, or consistent sounds (bell, chime) at countdown intervals. The power of transition songs lies in their consistency and predictability. When the same song plays every time it's time to clean up, the child's brain begins associating that music with transition. The song becomes a reliable predictor that change is coming. For children who are auditory processors or who find visual information overwhelming, audio cues may be more accessible. For many children, combining audio and visual countdowns provides multimodal support that reinforces the message through multiple channels. Audio cues also work when the child isn't looking at a timer — the sound reaches them regardless of attention direction. Price: ₹100–500 (speakers/devices); songs free or minimal cost. DIY option: Creating audio countdown systems: 1) CHOOSE CONSISTENT SONGS: select specific songs for specific transitions — one for clean-up, one for leaving, one for bedtime; the same song = the same transition, always, 2) LENGTH MATTERS: transition song should be long enough to complete the transition (2-3 minutes for clean-up) but not so long the transition drags, 3) BUILD-IN COUNTDOWN: some songs count down naturally; you can also create verbal countdown recordings — 'Five minutes left... four minutes left...', 4) MULTI-SENSORY PAIRING: pair audio countdown with visual timer — when child hears 'one minute left,' they can check the timer and see one minute remaining, 5) WARNING SOUND: use a consistent warning sound (bell, chime) to indicate countdown is starting — 'When you hear the bell, we have 5 minutes left,' 6) AUDIO TIMER APPS: some timer apps announce time remaining at intervals; set to give audio warnings at 5 minutes, 2 minutes, 1 minute, 7) TRANSITION PLAYLIST: create a playlist that naturally leads to transition — energetic song during activity, slower song as countdown, specific ending song that means 'all done now,' 8) MAKE IT FUN: transition songs should feel like part of the routine, not punishment; upbeat, pleasant songs work better than jarring alarms, 9) TEACH THE ASSOCIATION: explicitly teach what the song means — 'When you hear this song, it means clean-up time. Let's practice!', 10) CONSISTENT USE: the more consistently the same song signals the same transition, the more reliable the association becomes in the child's brain. SONG IDEAS: 'Clean Up' song for cleaning; 'Time to Go' songs for leaving; countdown songs (rocket ship countdown, etc.); custom songs recorded by parent with specific instructions. Safety: Avoid harsh, alarming sounds for transitions — these can trigger fight-or-flight rather than smooth transition. Use pleasant, predictable sounds that the child learns to associate with safe transitions.

mainEntity.name: How does a Transition Objects and Comfort Items help with transition difficulties?

acceptedAnswer.text: Transition objects are items that the child carries from one activity or location to another, providing continuity and comfort during the change. For children who struggle with transitions, the ending of one activity and beginning of another can feel like a complete break — as if everything is changing at once. A transition object provides something constant across the change, reducing the feeling of total disruption. Transition objects can be: comfort items the child already loves (stuffed animal, blanket, special toy), functional items that serve a purpose in both settings (backpack that goes from home to school), or special transition items used specifically to ease transitions (a 'transition star' that travels with the child). The object becomes an anchor of sameness in a changing environment. For some children, the transition object also serves as a regulation tool — something to hold, squeeze, or comfort themselves with during the stressful moment of change. Transition objects work particularly well for location-based transitions (home to school, inside to outside, one room to another) where the child can carry the object from one place to the next. The object's presence signals that even though the activity and location are changing, something remains constant and safe. Price: ₹100–1,000. DIY option: Implementing transition objects: 1) IDENTIFY NATURAL OBJECTS: does the child already have a comfort item they're attached to? This can become an official transition helper, 2) CREATE A TRANSITION HELPER: introduce a special item specifically for transitions — a small toy, stress ball, or object that 'helps with changes,' 3) NAME IT: give the transition object a name or role — 'This is your transition buddy. He comes with you when things change,' 4) CONSISTENT USE: the same object should be used for transitions consistently; don't switch objects frequently, 5) SMALL AND PORTABLE: transition objects should be small enough to carry easily and appropriate for various settings, 6) NOT A REWARD: the transition object isn't earned or taken away for behavior; it's a support tool that's always available, 7) INTRODUCE BEFORE TRANSITION: 'Here's your transition buddy. The timer shows we have this much time left, and then we'll go to snack. Your buddy will come with you,' 8) BRIDGE ACTIVITIES: child holds object during current activity AND carries it to next activity; the object bridges the gap, 9) GRADUAL FADING: as transition skills improve, gradually reduce reliance on transition objects if appropriate; some children may always benefit from transition supports, 10) SCHOOL COORDINATION: if transition objects help, coordinate with school — can the object stay in the child's backpack or cubby for use during difficult transitions? TYPES OF TRANSITION OBJECTS: comfort items (stuffed animals, blankets), sensory items (stress balls, fidgets), symbolic items (special bracelet, 'brave stone'), functional items (backpack, water bottle — things that naturally travel between settings). Safety: Ensure transition objects are safe and appropriate for all settings. Objects should not become so essential that their absence causes meltdowns — build in flexibility. Check school policies regarding personal items.

mainEntity.name: How does a Social Stories™ for Transitions and Countdowns help with transition difficulties?

acceptedAnswer.text: Social Stories about transitions explain what happens during countdown and transition processes, why countdowns are used, how to respond when time is up, and what to expect in the next activity. For children who don't intuitively understand the purpose of countdowns or struggle with the expectations around transitions, Social Stories provide explicit explanation in a child-friendly format. A Social Story about countdowns might explain: 'Sometimes I'm doing something fun and I don't want to stop. Mom uses a timer to help me know when time is almost up. When I can see the timer getting smaller, that means time is ending soon. When the timer is all done, it's time to stop. I might feel sad that the activity is over. That's okay. I can take a breath and remember that we'll do fun things again. Stopping when the timer ends helps everyone, and I can try to do it.' Social Stories normalize the feelings associated with transitions (frustration, sadness), explain the tools being used (timers, First-Then boards), and provide guidance on expected behavior. They can also introduce new countdown tools before they're implemented, helping the child understand what the tool is and how it will work. Price: ₹500–2,000 (books); DIY free. DIY option: Writing Social Stories for countdowns and transitions: 1) IDENTIFY THE CHALLENGE: what specific countdown/transition is difficult? (Leaving the park, ending screen time, switching between activities, etc.), 2) WRITE FROM CHILD'S PERSPECTIVE: first person, present tense — 'Sometimes I am playing and I don't want to stop...', 3) EXPLAIN THE TOOL: 'My mom uses a timer to help me. The timer shows how much time is left. When the red part gets smaller, time is almost done,' 4) ACKNOWLEDGE FEELINGS: 'I might feel frustrated when time is up. That's okay. Lots of kids feel this way,' 5) PROVIDE EXPECTED BEHAVIOR: 'When the timer beeps, I can stop what I'm doing. I can take a deep breath. I can go to the next activity,' 6) INCLUDE POSITIVE OUTCOME: 'When I stop nicely, I feel proud. Mom is happy. I will get to play again later,' 7) USE PICTURES: add illustrations or photos — the timer, the child transitioning, the next activity, emotions, 8) RATIO: 2-5 descriptive/perspective sentences per 1 directive sentence (don't make it all instructions), 9) READ BEFORE TRANSITIONS: read the Social Story before challenging transitions — in the morning before school, before going to the park, during calm moments, 10) UPDATE AS NEEDED: revise stories as countdown systems change or child's understanding develops. TRANSITION STORY TOPICS: why we use timers, what happens when activities end, going from home to school, leaving the playground, ending screen time, coping with unexpected changes, waiting and patience. Safety: Social Stories should explain, not threaten. Avoid punitive language ('If you don't stop, you'll lose...'). Stories should build understanding and provide support, not create fear of consequences.

@graph.name: How to Help a Child with Transition Difficulties
@graph.description: A graded approach using evidence-based materials. Countdown strategies address time blindness — the inability to perceive, estimate, or track the passage of time that is common in autism, ADHD, and other conditions affecting executive function. For children with time blindness, verbal time warnings ('five more minutes') are essentially meaningless because the words don't connect to any internal sense of time passing. These children cannot feel time flowing, cannot estimate how long five minutes is, and cannot mentally prepare for a transition they cannot perceive approaching. The result is that every transition feels sudden and surprising, even when extensive verbal warnings were given. Countdown materials work by externalizing time — making the invisible visible. When a child can SEE time running out (visual timer), SEE what's coming next (First-Then board), and SEE progress toward the end (countdown strip), they can begin the cognitive and emotional preparation for transition that other children do automatically based on internal time sense. The goal of countdown strategies is to provide the advance warning that allows the brain to shift gears smoothly. Without visible countdown, the child's brain is in the current activity until — suddenly — it's demanded to be somewhere else. With visible countdown, the brain can gradually disengage, process the upcoming change, and prepare for the shift. Effective countdown intervention combines multiple elements: visual representation of time, knowledge of what comes next, regulation support for the moment of change, and consistent implementation across settings. The materials in this reel provide the building blocks for a comprehensive countdown system.

supply.name: Visual Timers (Time Timer®, Sand Timers, Visual Countdown Clocks)
Countdown Visual Strips and Number Countdowns

step.name: Understand the Problem
step.text: Countdown strategies are structured approaches to helping children understand, anticipate, and cope with the passage of time and upcoming transitions. Many children, particularly those with autism spectrum disorder, ADHD, anxiety, or executive function challenges, have difficulty with temporal processing — the ability to perceive, estimate, and track the passage of time. This 'time blindness' means that verbal time warnings ('five more minutes') have little meaning because the child cannot connect abstract time units to felt experience. Countdowns make time visible and concrete, providing the advance warning that allows the child's brain to prepare for change. Effective countdown strategies typically involve: visual representation of time passing (timers that show time disappearing), auditory or visual cues at intervals, concrete markers of progress toward transition, and consistent use across settings. When children can SEE time running out, they can begin the mental process of disengaging from the current activity and preparing for the next. Without visible countdowns, transitions feel sudden and jarring — even when verbal warnings were given — because the child's internal sense of time didn't register those warnings. Countdown materials range from simple visual timers to elaborate transition systems, but they share the common goal of making the invisible (time) visible. Countdown strategies address time blindness — the inability to perceive, estimate, or track the passage of time that is common in autism, ADHD, and other conditions affecting executive function. For children with time blindness, verbal time warnings ('five more minutes') are essentially meaningless because the words don't connect to any internal sense of time passing. These children cannot feel time flowing, cannot estimate how long five minutes is, and cannot mentally prepare for a transition they cannot perceive approaching. The result is that every transition feels sudden and surprising, even when extensive verbal warnings were given. Countdown materials work by externalizing time — making the invisible visible. When a child can SEE time running out (visual timer), SEE what's coming next (First-Then board), and SEE progress toward the end (countdown strip), they can begin the cognitive and emotional preparation for transition that other children do automatically based on internal time sense. The goal of countdown strategies is to provide the advance warning that allows the brain to shift gears smoothly. Without visible countdown, the child's brain is in the current activity until — suddenly — it's demanded to be somewhere else. With visible countdown, the brain can gradually disengage, process the upcoming change, and prepare for the shift. Effective countdown intervention combines multiple elements: visual representation of time, knowledge of what comes next, regulation support for the moment of change, and consistent implementation across settings. The materials in this reel provide the building blocks for a comprehensive countdown system.

step.name: Start with Foundation Materials
step.text: Begin with: Visual Timers (Time Timer®, Sand Timers, Visual Countdown Clocks), First-Then Boards and Visual Schedules, Countdown Visual Strips and Number Countdowns.

signOrSymptom.name: Meltdowns when activities end, even with verbal warnings given
No apparent understanding of time concepts ('five minutes,' 'soon,' 'later')
Difficulty stopping preferred activities to move to non-preferred ones
Seems surprised by transitions even when warned repeatedly
Cannot estimate how long activities will take
Gets 'stuck' on activities and can't shift attention
Resists any change in routine or activity
Asks repeatedly 'how much longer?' without understanding answers

@graph.name: 9 Materials for Transition Difficulties
@graph.description: Evidence-based materials for ages 2–12 years (with materials adapted for developmental level and time perception ability)

item.name: Visual Timers (Time Timer®, Sand Timers, Visual Countdown Clocks)
item.description: Visual timers are the foundation of countdown strategies because they make the abstract concrete. The Time Timer® and similar visual timers show time as a colored disk that disappears as time passes — children can literally SEE time running out. Sand timers show sand flowing from one chamber to another, providing both visual and kinesthetic awareness of time passing. Unlike digital clocks that show numbers (abstract), visual timers show quantity of time remaining (concrete). For children with time blindness, this is transformative. When a child can see that the red section is getting smaller, they understand viscerally that the activity is ending soon. Their brain can begin preparing for the transition before it happens, rather than being surprised when time is 'suddenly' up. Visual timers work because they bypass the need for time estimation (a skill the child lacks) and provide direct perception of time remaining. They create predictability without requiring the child to hold abstract time concepts in working memory. The child doesn't need to understand what 'five minutes' means — they just need to watch the color disappear. This simple intervention alone can dramatically reduce transition meltdowns by giving the child's brain the advance warning it needs to shift gears.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Using visual timers effectively: 1) CHOOSE THE RIGHT TIMER: Time Timer® (classic visual timer with disappearing red disk) is the gold standard; sand timers work well for shorter durations (1-5 minutes) and add sensory element; liquid motion timers for calming visual effect; phone/tablet apps work but dedicated devices avoid screen distractions, 2) MATCH DURATION TO TASK: use appropriate time length — don't set a 30-minute timer for a 5-minute countdown; start with the time that matches the child's tolerance, 3) PLACEMENT: timer should be visible to child during the activity — on the table, on the wall, wherever they can see it while playing, 4) INTRODUCE THE CONCEPT: before using in high-stakes situations, teach what the timer means — 'When the red is gone, we're all done' — practice with preferred activities first, 5) PAIR WITH LANGUAGE: 'Look, the timer shows we have this much time left (pointing). When it's all gone, we'll clean up,' 6) CONSISTENT USE: use the same timer consistently so child learns to trust it; inconsistent use undermines effectiveness, 7) HONOR THE TIMER: when the timer goes off, the activity ends — don't regularly extend time or the timer loses meaning, 8) START WITH PREFERRED-TO-PREFERRED: first practice transitioning from one preferred activity to another; once child trusts the system, use for preferred-to-non-preferred transitions, 9) MULTIPLE WARNINGS: set timer for full duration, then point out time remaining at intervals — 'Look, we have this much left... now this much...', 10) PORTABLE TIMERS: keep a portable timer for use outside the home — parks, stores, playdates. COMMON MISTAKES: setting timer for too long (child forgets about it); using timer inconsistently; not honoring the timer when it ends; using timer only for non-preferred activities (creates negative association).

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: For some anxious children, watching time run out can increase anxiety rather than reduce it. Monitor child's response and adjust approach if timer creates stress rather than support.

item.name: First-Then Boards and Visual Schedules
item.description: First-Then boards show two activities in sequence: 'FIRST [current activity], THEN [next activity].' This simple visual tool helps children understand that the current activity will end AND shows them what comes next. Knowing what's coming reduces the anxiety that often underlies transition resistance. Many children resist transitions not because they can't handle ending the current activity, but because they're anxious about the unknown — what happens after? First-Then boards answer that question visually. Visual schedules extend this concept to show the entire sequence of activities (morning routine, school day, after-school schedule). Children can see where they are in the sequence and what's coming, reducing the surprise factor of transitions. The visual format is critical because it provides concrete, persistent information — unlike verbal instructions that disappear after being spoken. A child can look at the First-Then board repeatedly, referencing it to prepare for the transition. For countdown purposes, First-Then boards are often paired with visual timers: 'First we play for this much time (timer), THEN we have snack.' The combination provides both the 'when' (timer) and the 'what's next' (First-Then board), giving the child complete transition information.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Creating and using First-Then boards: 1) MAKE THE BOARD: simple board with two sections labeled 'FIRST' and 'THEN'; can use whiteboard, laminated card, felt board, or purchased board; Velcro spots for interchangeable pictures, 2) PICTURE CARDS: create picture cards for common activities — playing, eating, bathing, school, outside, TV, reading, etc.; use real photos for younger children or those who need concrete images; simple icons work for older children, 3) SET UP BEFORE TRANSITION: place First-Then board before the current activity begins; child sees from the start what's coming, 4) REVIEW VERBALLY: 'Look at your board. FIRST we're playing with blocks. THEN we're having snack,' 5) REFERENCE DURING COUNTDOWN: 'The timer shows we have this much time for FIRST. Then we'll do THEN,' 6) MARK COMPLETION: when first activity ends, move or remove the FIRST picture; visually mark the transition happening, 7) MOTIVATING 'THEN': especially for difficult transitions, make THEN something motivating — 'FIRST cleanup, THEN iPad time,' 8) CONSISTENT PLACEMENT: keep board in consistent location so child knows where to look, 9) EXPAND TO SCHEDULES: as child develops, expand to visual schedules showing more activities in sequence; use for daily routines, school day, weekend plans, 10) PORTABLE VERSION: create small, portable First-Then board for use outside home. PAIRING WITH COUNTDOWN: First-Then shows WHAT; visual timer shows WHEN. Together they provide complete transition information. 'FIRST play (timer shows 5 minutes). THEN snack.'

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: If THEN activity is aversive (e.g., doctor appointment), child may resist the entire sequence. For challenging THEN activities, use smaller First-Then chains and add motivators after the hard activity.

item.name: Countdown Visual Strips and Number Countdowns
item.description: Countdown visual strips provide a structured visual sequence that marks progress toward transition. They might show numbers (5-4-3-2-1-Done), pictures (5 turns left, 4 turns left...), or other visual markers that the child removes or marks off as the countdown progresses. For some children, visual timers that show continuous time (like the Time Timer) are less effective than discrete countdowns — seeing specific steps toward the end rather than a gradual disappearing segment. Countdown strips make each step distinct: 'Here's 5. Now I'm removing 5. Here's 4. Now I'm removing 4...' The child participates in the countdown by removing numbers or marking off steps, which increases engagement and gives them a sense of control over the transition process. This participatory element is key — the child isn't just watching time happen to them, they're actively participating in the countdown. Countdown strips can represent time (each number = 1 minute), turns (each number = 1 turn in a game), or sequences (5 more swings, 4 more swings...). The flexibility allows matching to the child's understanding — some children grasp 'turns' better than 'minutes.' The visual-motor act of removing cards or marking off numbers also provides a concrete, memorable marker of progress.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Creating and using countdown strips: 1) MAKE THE STRIP: laminated strip with Velcro spaces for numbers or pictures; numbers 5-4-3-2-1 plus 'ALL DONE' or 'FINISHED', 2) VELCRO NUMBERS: individual number cards that attach to the strip and can be removed one by one, 3) DETERMINE WHAT EACH NUMBER MEANS: time-based (each number = 1 minute); turn-based (each number = 1 turn); item-based (5 more crackers, 4 more crackers...); the most effective choice depends on the child's understanding, 4) INTRODUCE THE SYSTEM: 'We're going to count down. When all the numbers are gone, we're all done and we'll go inside,' 5) CHILD REMOVES NUMBERS: at each countdown point, the child removes the number from the strip; the active participation increases investment in the process, 6) VERBAL PAIRING: 'That's 5. Now let's take off 5. Now we have 4-3-2-1 left,' 7) CLEAR ENDING: when all numbers are removed, the strip shows 'ALL DONE' or is empty; this visual endpoint is unambiguous, 8) ADJUST LENGTH: start with shorter countdowns (3 or 5) and extend as child builds tolerance; some children need 10-step countdowns for longer activities, 9) PAIR WITH FIRST-THEN: countdown shows WHEN we're done; First-Then shows WHAT'S NEXT, 10) TURN-BASED COUNTDOWNS: for activities where time doesn't apply well (games, swings), countdown turns instead — '5 more turns, 4 more turns...'. VARIATIONS: instead of numbers, use pictures (5 smiley faces disappearing); use tokens child drops in a container; use a path where figure moves toward 'finish.' Key is discrete, visual progress toward defined endpoint.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Some children become anxious watching numbers count down and may resist participation. If this occurs, consider whether continuous visual timers work better than discrete countdowns for this particular child.

item.name: Transition Songs and Audio Countdown Cues
item.description: Some children respond better to auditory countdown cues than visual ones — or benefit from having both. Transition songs are consistent musical cues that signal the approach of a transition or mark the transition itself. The famous 'Clean Up Song' is an example of a transition song that signals activity ending. Audio countdowns can include: songs with built-in countdowns ('5 little monkeys' counting down), verbal countdown routines set to music, audio timers that give periodic warnings, or consistent sounds (bell, chime) at countdown intervals. The power of transition songs lies in their consistency and predictability. When the same song plays every time it's time to clean up, the child's brain begins associating that music with transition. The song becomes a reliable predictor that change is coming. For children who are auditory processors or who find visual information overwhelming, audio cues may be more accessible. For many children, combining audio and visual countdowns provides multimodal support that reinforces the message through multiple channels. Audio cues also work when the child isn't looking at a timer — the sound reaches them regardless of attention direction.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Creating audio countdown systems: 1) CHOOSE CONSISTENT SONGS: select specific songs for specific transitions — one for clean-up, one for leaving, one for bedtime; the same song = the same transition, always, 2) LENGTH MATTERS: transition song should be long enough to complete the transition (2-3 minutes for clean-up) but not so long the transition drags, 3) BUILD-IN COUNTDOWN: some songs count down naturally; you can also create verbal countdown recordings — 'Five minutes left... four minutes left...', 4) MULTI-SENSORY PAIRING: pair audio countdown with visual timer — when child hears 'one minute left,' they can check the timer and see one minute remaining, 5) WARNING SOUND: use a consistent warning sound (bell, chime) to indicate countdown is starting — 'When you hear the bell, we have 5 minutes left,' 6) AUDIO TIMER APPS: some timer apps announce time remaining at intervals; set to give audio warnings at 5 minutes, 2 minutes, 1 minute, 7) TRANSITION PLAYLIST: create a playlist that naturally leads to transition — energetic song during activity, slower song as countdown, specific ending song that means 'all done now,' 8) MAKE IT FUN: transition songs should feel like part of the routine, not punishment; upbeat, pleasant songs work better than jarring alarms, 9) TEACH THE ASSOCIATION: explicitly teach what the song means — 'When you hear this song, it means clean-up time. Let's practice!', 10) CONSISTENT USE: the more consistently the same song signals the same transition, the more reliable the association becomes in the child's brain. SONG IDEAS: 'Clean Up' song for cleaning; 'Time to Go' songs for leaving; countdown songs (rocket ship countdown, etc.); custom songs recorded by parent with specific instructions.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Avoid harsh, alarming sounds for transitions — these can trigger fight-or-flight rather than smooth transition. Use pleasant, predictable sounds that the child learns to associate with safe transitions.

item.name: Transition Objects and Comfort Items
item.description: Transition objects are items that the child carries from one activity or location to another, providing continuity and comfort during the change. For children who struggle with transitions, the ending of one activity and beginning of another can feel like a complete break — as if everything is changing at once. A transition object provides something constant across the change, reducing the feeling of total disruption. Transition objects can be: comfort items the child already loves (stuffed animal, blanket, special toy), functional items that serve a purpose in both settings (backpack that goes from home to school), or special transition items used specifically to ease transitions (a 'transition star' that travels with the child). The object becomes an anchor of sameness in a changing environment. For some children, the transition object also serves as a regulation tool — something to hold, squeeze, or comfort themselves with during the stressful moment of change. Transition objects work particularly well for location-based transitions (home to school, inside to outside, one room to another) where the child can carry the object from one place to the next. The object's presence signals that even though the activity and location are changing, something remains constant and safe.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Implementing transition objects: 1) IDENTIFY NATURAL OBJECTS: does the child already have a comfort item they're attached to? This can become an official transition helper, 2) CREATE A TRANSITION HELPER: introduce a special item specifically for transitions — a small toy, stress ball, or object that 'helps with changes,' 3) NAME IT: give the transition object a name or role — 'This is your transition buddy. He comes with you when things change,' 4) CONSISTENT USE: the same object should be used for transitions consistently; don't switch objects frequently, 5) SMALL AND PORTABLE: transition objects should be small enough to carry easily and appropriate for various settings, 6) NOT A REWARD: the transition object isn't earned or taken away for behavior; it's a support tool that's always available, 7) INTRODUCE BEFORE TRANSITION: 'Here's your transition buddy. The timer shows we have this much time left, and then we'll go to snack. Your buddy will come with you,' 8) BRIDGE ACTIVITIES: child holds object during current activity AND carries it to next activity; the object bridges the gap, 9) GRADUAL FADING: as transition skills improve, gradually reduce reliance on transition objects if appropriate; some children may always benefit from transition supports, 10) SCHOOL COORDINATION: if transition objects help, coordinate with school — can the object stay in the child's backpack or cubby for use during difficult transitions? TYPES OF TRANSITION OBJECTS: comfort items (stuffed animals, blankets), sensory items (stress balls, fidgets), symbolic items (special bracelet, 'brave stone'), functional items (backpack, water bottle — things that naturally travel between settings).

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Ensure transition objects are safe and appropriate for all settings. Objects should not become so essential that their absence causes meltdowns — build in flexibility. Check school policies regarding personal items.

item.name: Social Stories™ for Transitions and Countdowns
item.description: Social Stories about transitions explain what happens during countdown and transition processes, why countdowns are used, how to respond when time is up, and what to expect in the next activity. For children who don't intuitively understand the purpose of countdowns or struggle with the expectations around transitions, Social Stories provide explicit explanation in a child-friendly format. A Social Story about countdowns might explain: 'Sometimes I'm doing something fun and I don't want to stop. Mom uses a timer to help me know when time is almost up. When I can see the timer getting smaller, that means time is ending soon. When the timer is all done, it's time to stop. I might feel sad that the activity is over. That's okay. I can take a breath and remember that we'll do fun things again. Stopping when the timer ends helps everyone, and I can try to do it.' Social Stories normalize the feelings associated with transitions (frustration, sadness), explain the tools being used (timers, First-Then boards), and provide guidance on expected behavior. They can also introduce new countdown tools before they're implemented, helping the child understand what the tool is and how it will work.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Writing Social Stories for countdowns and transitions: 1) IDENTIFY THE CHALLENGE: what specific countdown/transition is difficult? (Leaving the park, ending screen time, switching between activities, etc.), 2) WRITE FROM CHILD'S PERSPECTIVE: first person, present tense — 'Sometimes I am playing and I don't want to stop...', 3) EXPLAIN THE TOOL: 'My mom uses a timer to help me. The timer shows how much time is left. When the red part gets smaller, time is almost done,' 4) ACKNOWLEDGE FEELINGS: 'I might feel frustrated when time is up. That's okay. Lots of kids feel this way,' 5) PROVIDE EXPECTED BEHAVIOR: 'When the timer beeps, I can stop what I'm doing. I can take a deep breath. I can go to the next activity,' 6) INCLUDE POSITIVE OUTCOME: 'When I stop nicely, I feel proud. Mom is happy. I will get to play again later,' 7) USE PICTURES: add illustrations or photos — the timer, the child transitioning, the next activity, emotions, 8) RATIO: 2-5 descriptive/perspective sentences per 1 directive sentence (don't make it all instructions), 9) READ BEFORE TRANSITIONS: read the Social Story before challenging transitions — in the morning before school, before going to the park, during calm moments, 10) UPDATE AS NEEDED: revise stories as countdown systems change or child's understanding develops. TRANSITION STORY TOPICS: why we use timers, what happens when activities end, going from home to school, leaving the playground, ending screen time, coping with unexpected changes, waiting and patience.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Social Stories should explain, not threaten. Avoid punitive language ('If you don't stop, you'll lose...'). Stories should build understanding and provide support, not create fear of consequences.

item.name: Token Systems and Countdown Token Boards
item.description: Token systems use tangible tokens (coins, stars, check marks) that accumulate or count down to represent progress. For countdown purposes, token boards work in reverse — instead of earning tokens toward a goal, tokens are removed as time passes, visually representing the countdown. A countdown token board might start with 5 tokens; every minute (or every turn, or every segment of activity), one token is removed. When all tokens are gone, the activity is finished. This gives children a concrete, manipulable representation of time passing. The physical nature of removing tokens provides sensory feedback and requires the child's participation. Some children respond to the tactile, tangible nature of tokens better than to visual timers where time just disappears. The act of moving, removing, or transferring tokens creates distinct moments in the countdown rather than a continuous flow. Token systems can also combine countdown and motivation: 'You have 5 tokens. Each token is one minute. When all the tokens are in the 'finished' jar, we're done. If you transition nicely, you can earn a new token for next time.' This adds a reward component while maintaining the countdown structure.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Creating countdown token systems: 1) CHOOSE TOKENS: poker chips, coins, stars, stickers, small toys — anything tangible and uniform; tokens should be sturdy enough for repeated use, 2) CREATE THE BOARD: board with spots for tokens (Velcro, slots, or simply a line-up); 'FINISHED' container or area for removed tokens, 3) DETERMINE COUNTDOWN VALUE: each token = 1 minute, 1 turn, 1 activity segment; make the conversion clear to the child, 4) START FULL: begin with all tokens on the board — 'Look, we have 5 tokens. Each token is one minute of play time,' 5) CHILD REMOVES TOKENS: at each interval, child moves one token to the 'finished' container; active participation increases engagement, 6) VISUAL PROGRESS: as tokens are removed, the board visually shows fewer remaining — the countdown is obvious, 7) CLEAR ENDPOINT: when all tokens are in the finished container, the activity ends; no negotiation, no ambiguity, 8) CONSISTENT LANGUAGE: 'One token down, four left. One token down, three left...' — pair visual countdown with verbal countdown, 9) PAIR WITH MOTIVATION: 'If you transition nicely when all tokens are done, you'll earn a bonus token for tomorrow' — not required but can help with particularly difficult transitions, 10) ADJUST QUANTITY: start with fewer tokens (3-5) for children new to the system; increase as tolerance develops. VARIATIONS: countdown jar (remove items from jar); countdown chain (remove links from paper chain); countdown path (figure moves along path to finish); countdown cups (flip cups as time passes).

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Token boards should support transitions, not create anxiety about losing tokens. Present tokens as helpful countdown tools, not as things being taken away.

item.name: Countdown Visual Apps and Digital Tools
item.description: Digital countdown apps bring visual timer concepts to tablets and phones, often with additional features like customization, sounds, animations, and data tracking. Many children are highly motivated by technology, making app-based countdown tools engaging where traditional timers might be ignored. Digital countdown tools include: visual timer apps (Time Timer app, Visual Timer apps), countdown apps with animations (rocket ship launching, character moving), customizable countdown sequences, apps that announce time remaining verbally, and apps that pair visual countdown with First-Then functionality. The advantages of digital tools include: always available on devices you already have, easy customization to child's interests (themes, sounds, rewards), ability to save different timer presets for different situations, portability without extra items to carry, and engaging presentation that captures attention. For some children, particularly those motivated by screens and technology, digital countdown tools are more effective than physical timers. The gamification elements (animations, rewards, sounds) can make countdown and transition feel more positive and engaging.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Using digital countdown tools effectively: 1) TIME TIMER APP: official app version of the Time Timer; maintains the familiar disappearing-disk visual on phone/tablet, 2) VISUAL COUNTDOWN APPS: many apps show time counting down visually; choose one with clear, uncluttered visual display, 3) ANIMATED COUNTDOWN APPS: for engagement-motivated children, apps with animations (rockets, characters, games) can increase attention to countdown, 4) AUDIO ANNOUNCEMENT APPS: apps that verbally announce time remaining at intervals; useful when child isn't watching screen, 5) CUSTOMIZATION: choose apps that allow customization — colors, sounds, intervals — to match child's preferences and needs, 6) SEPARATE FROM PLAY DEVICE: if possible, use a dedicated device for timer apps so the countdown isn't competing with other apps; if using same device, close other apps, 7) CONSISTENT APP: pick one countdown app and use it consistently rather than switching between different apps, 8) PAIR WITH PHYSICAL STRATEGIES: digital countdown can supplement, not replace, other strategies; pair with First-Then boards, transition objects, etc., 9) TECH TRANSITION CHALLENGE: screen time is often the HARDEST transition; using a screen-based countdown to end screen time can be tricky — may need non-screen countdown for this specific transition, 10) FREE VS. PAID: many quality countdown apps are free or low-cost; try free versions before purchasing. RECOMMENDED APPS: Time Timer, Visual Timer, Kids Timer, Countdown Star, First Then Visual Schedule (includes timer); search app store for 'visual timer' or 'countdown timer for kids' and try options. CAUTION: Ensure app is actually used for countdown, not as a gateway to other device use.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Screen-based tools may not be appropriate for all children or situations. Avoid using device for countdown if the device itself is a source of transition difficulty (ending screen time). Monitor for appropriate use.

item.name: Calm-Down Tools and Regulation Supports for Transition Moments
item.description: Even with the best countdown strategies, the moment of transition can still be dysregulating. Calm-down tools and regulation supports provide the child with strategies for managing the difficult feelings that arise when activities end and transitions occur. These might include: deep breathing visual cues (star breathing, square breathing), sensory tools (stress balls, fidgets, weighted items), calming scripts or self-talk cards ('I can do hard things'), physical regulation strategies (heavy work, wall push-ups), and calm-down corner setups. The purpose is to help the child regulate their emotional response to the transition, not just to prepare them for it. A child might watch the countdown, understand time is ending, and still feel a surge of frustration, sadness, or anxiety when the timer goes off. Regulation tools give them something to DO with those feelings rather than melting down. For successful transition, many children need the full package: countdown awareness (timer) + knowledge of what's next (First-Then) + regulation support (calm-down tools). The countdown prepares them cognitively, but the calm-down tools prepare them emotionally to handle the moment of change.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Building regulation support into countdown systems: 1) ANTICIPATE THE MOMENT: know that the transition moment — when timer ends — is the hardest part; prepare for it, 2) BREATHING VISUALS: teach and practice deep breathing; have visual cue (star breathing, square breathing poster) ready to use at transition time, 3) SENSORY TOOLS READY: stress ball, fidget, or calming sensory item available AT the transition moment; child can squeeze or hold during the shift, 4) SELF-TALK CUES: simple cards with calming phrases — 'I can do hard things,' 'This feeling will pass,' 'The activity will come back,' — child can read or be reminded of these during transition, 5) TRANSITION RITUAL: build a calming ritual into transitions — three deep breaths, squeeze stress ball, say 'I'm ready' — a mini-regulation routine, 6) MOVEMENT BREAK: some children regulate through movement; a brief physical activity (jumping jacks, wall push-ups, march in place) between activities can help, 7) COMFORT ITEM ACCESS: ensure transition object or comfort item is accessible at the transition moment, 8) PREVIEW THE REGULATION: 'The timer will go off soon. When it does, we'll take three deep breaths together, and then we'll go to snack,' 9) PRACTICE WHEN CALM: teach regulation strategies during calm moments; then apply them during transitions; strategies taught in crisis don't work well, 10) ZONES CHECK-IN: if using Zones of Regulation, check in with zone during countdown — 'You're looking yellow. Let's do some breathing before the timer ends to get to green.' COMBINATION APPROACH: the most effective transition system combines: visual countdown (awareness of time) + First-Then (knowledge of what's next) + calm-down tools (regulation for the transition moment) + positive reinforcement (motivation for successful transition).

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Regulation strategies must be taught during calm times before being applied during transitions. Children cannot learn new strategies in moments of dysregulation — they can only access strategies they've already practiced.

socialMedia.caption: When 'five more minutes' means nothing and every transition is a meltdown — your child isn't being defiant. They genuinely can't perceive time passing. Countdown materials make the invisible visible. Here are 9 that help: visual timers, First-Then boards, countdown strips, transition songs, transition objects, Social Stories, token boards, countdown apps, and calm-down tools. From ambush to anticipation. From meltdown to smooth transition. 💜 #TransitionStrategies #VisualTimer #CountdownSupport #PinnacleBlooms
